{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"Thus, Mr Justice French agreed with the interpretation of the Human Rights Commission and the primary judge who decided that section 18D \"may be seen as defining the limits of the proscription in s 18C and not as a free speech exception to it. This is a clear interpretive ruling on the meaning and reach of those legal provisions."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"the search, despite being conducted without a warrant, was permissible under the common law. The Supreme Court supplemented this finding when it reasoned that based on the facts presented in the record, the search was neither expansive nor abusive. In doing so, the Supreme Court interestingly made it a point to reconcile the common law grounds for instituting a valid search and the rights guaranteed to Fearon under the Charter.","constitutional_review_explanation":"The Court did not strike down or uphold the validity of a statute; instead, it interpreted and clarified the scope of the common law power to conduct searches incident to arrest and reconciled that power with Charter rights, thereby engaging in interpretive review of the applicable legal framework."}

The court only applied existing statutory provisions; it did not invalidate, uphold, or interpret them in a way that meets the task’s criteria."}

Hence, this is classified as an interpretation of the legal provision."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"The Court began by clarifying that the expression \"political opinion\" in section 772(1)(f) should be construed broadly","constitutional_review_explanation":"The court did not strike down or uphold the validity of any provision, but it explicitly clarified and construed the meaning and scope of section 772(1)(f) of the Fair Work Act (and relevant enterprise agreement clauses), which constitutes an interpretive review of the law."}

{"constitutional_review":"invalidated","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"\"Consequently, the court deemed sections 164(a) and (c), and 165 of the Penal Code to be unconstitutional and ordered the word \"private\" to be removed from section 167.\"","constitutional_review_explanation":"The High Court explicitly reviewed provisions of the Penal Code, declared sections 164(a) and (c) and 165 unconstitutional (thereby invalidating them), and severed the word \"private\" from section 167, which is an exercise of constitutional review invalidating and modifying the law."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"\"although Azerbaijan amended its laws pertinent to state registration of entities, the ECtHR reviewed the domestic law as it was applicable in the time of the case\"","constitutional_review_explanation":"The Court examined and applied the meaning and operation of Azerbaijan’s domestic registration laws (e.g., time limits and protection against delays) to determine whether interference was ‘prescribed by law’ under Article 11, without declaring those laws invalid or expressly upholding their constitutionality. This constitutes an interpretation of the relevant legal provisions."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"issued a ruling asking the government why the investigation against him should not be declared illegal and contrary to the Constitution of Bangladesh and the Digital Security Act, 2018","constitutional_review_explanation":"The High Court explicitly questioned the legality of the investigation under constitutional and statutory standards, indicating a review of the legality/meaning of the governing legal framework without actually striking it down or affirming its constitutionality. The appellate decision mainly upheld a stay, so the outcome reflects an interpretive review rather than invalidation or upholding of a law."}

{"constitutional_review":"upheld","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"The Court concluded that while the EFED Act infringed on the implied freedom of political communication, the state of New South Wales had a legitimate purpose to prevent corruption and used legitimate means to do so with the contested provisions in the EFED Act.","constitutional_review_explanation":"The High Court explicitly assessed whether the EFED Act’s provisions unjustifiably infringed the implied constitutional freedom of political communication and determined that, despite the burden, the provisions had a legitimate purpose and used legitimate means. This amounts to an explicit constitutional review upholding the validity of the contested provisions."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"The Court emphasized that section 4(2)(b) of the PPPA considers \"the harm likely to have been or to be suffered by the respondent as a result of the application's expression .\" [para. 74] [emphasis in original] Thus, it concluded, \"loss of a right to sue is a possible outcome of the public weighing exercise, not an input.\"","constitutional_review_explanation":"The Supreme Court did not strike down or uphold the statute’s validity, but it expressly clarified the meaning and scope of section 4(2)(b) of the PPPA—especially what type of ‘harm’ is relevant in the public interest weighing exercise—so this is a statutory interpretation outcome."}

{"constitutional_review":null,"constitutional_review_status":"not_found","constitutional_review_evidence":"","constitutional_review_explanation":"The case discusses modification of the common law fair comment defense and its application to a defamation dispute, but there is no explicit review of the validity, constitutionality, or formal interpretation of a specific statute, regulation, or constitutional provision. The court changes common law doctrine, not the validity or meaning of a codified law, so no constitutional review outcome is identified."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"“In an application involving cyberbullying, there is no need for a child to demonstrate that he or she personally conforms to this legal paradigm. The law attributes the heightened vulnerability based on chronology, not temperament. While evidence of a direct, harmful consequence to an individual applicant is relevant, courts may also conclude that there is objectively discernable harm.”","constitutional_review_explanation":"The Supreme Court did not strike down or uphold the validity of any statute, but it expressly clarified how the law concerning children’s vulnerability and protection from cyberbullying should be understood and applied, interpreting the legal paradigm by explaining that heightened vulnerability is attributed based on age rather than individual temperament and that courts may rely on objectively discernible harm."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"the interference required examination under Article 10 to determine if it was prescribed by law","constitutional_review_explanation":"The Court did not strike down or uphold any specific domestic law as invalid or constitutional. Instead, it applied and interpreted the Article 10 ECHR requirement that interferences be ‘prescribed by law,’ assessing the quality, clarity, and safeguards of the Azerbaijani licensing framework. This is an interpretation of the Convention’s legal standard, not an invalidation or express upholding of a domestic statute."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"To assess if Meta's actions complied with its obligations under the International Human Rights Law [...] the OSB applied the three-part test that is provided by Article 19 of the ICCPR. According to this test, restrictions to freedom of expression can be valid as long as they are prescribed by law (legality), pursue a legitimate aim, and are necessary and proportionate.","constitutional_review_explanation":"The Oversight Board explicitly applied and worked through the Article 19 ICCPR three-part test (legality, legitimate aim, necessity and proportionality) to assess how restrictions on expression should operate. This is an explicit interpretation and application of the meaning and scope of a legal provision (Article 19 ICCPR), without declaring it invalid or expressly upholding its constitutionality, so it is classified as interpreted."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"The Court reasoned that the articles contravened the racial vilification provisions of Section 18C of the Racial Discrimination Act and because they contained erroneous facts, distortions of the truth and inflammatory and provocative language, they failed to meet the \"good faith\" requirement needed to satisfy  the exception to Section 18D","constitutional_review_explanation":"The court applied and clarified the meaning and requirements of Sections 18C and 18D of the Racial Discrimination Act, particularly the scope of the racial vilification provisions and the \"good faith\" exception, without invalidating or upholding the statute’s constitutionality. This constitutes interpretation of the legal provisions."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"It noted that, under the three-part test framework, any restriction on expression must be prescribed by law, pursue a legitimate aim, be necessary in a democratic society, and be proportionate.","constitutional_review_explanation":"The Board applied and interpreted the meaning and requirements of Article 19 ICCPR’s three-part test (legality, legitimate aim, necessity and proportionality) to Meta’s policies and their enforcement. This is an explicit interpretation of the scope and content of a legal provision, without invalidating or upholding a law’s constitutionality, so it is classified as “interpreted.”"}

The Court criticizes that domestic law permitted denial of access to the case file, yet it does not formally strike down, uphold, or interpret that law; it only applies Convention standards to the facts. Therefore, no constitutional or legal review outcome is identified."}

The court is applying existing legal tests, not assessing the validity or meaning of the underlying laws themselves in the constitutional or abstract sense required by the task."}

{"constitutional_review":"interpreted","constitutional_review_status":"found","constitutional_review_evidence":"the Amicus argued that the disclosure of all key points was necessary to save the constitutionality of section 10 of the NKPA since the provision and the related regulations laid down criminal sanctions for various acts related to national key points; however, if the citizenry did not know the places that were key points, then they could not be expected to avoid transgressions. Herein, the judge accepted the contentions put forth by the Amicus and observed that \"one of the central tenets underlying the common-law understanding of legality is that of foreseeability - that the rules of criminal law are clear and precise so that an individual may easily behave in a manner that avoids committing crimes\".","constitutional_review_explanation":"The court did not strike down or formally uphold section 10 of the NKPA, but accepted the amicus’s argument that disclosure of all key points was necessary to preserve its constitutionality. In doing so, the judge clarified and limited how section 10 must operate (i.e., in a manner consistent with foreseeability and legality), which is an interpretive constitutional review rather than invalidation or simple application."}
